coredata.dc:title: Portable and reusable HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+/polyacrylamide/sodium alginate for dynamic fluorescence switching detection of Fe3+ and secure information encryption

coredata.dc:description: Portable and reusable solid-state fluorescence sensors have received much attention in the field of metal ion detection as a superior alternative to traditional liquid-based fluorescence sensors. In this study, a novel HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+/polyacrylamide/sodium alginate (HLE/PS) hydrogel was successfully constructed for portable dynamic fluorescence switching detection of Fe3+ and encryption of security information. The HLE/PS hydrogel was synthesized from HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+ fluorescent nanofillers embedded in a semi-IPN network of polyacrylamide (PAM) and sodium alginate (SA). The HLE/PS hydrogel has demonstrated excellent performance in the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solutions with high selectivity, high sensitivity, fast response (8 min), wide detection range (2–1000 μM), low detection limit (0.78 μM) and ease of use. It can be reused up to five times, underlining its utility and economy as a good performance fluorescence sensor. Notably, the quenching of Fe3+ fluorescence by ultrasmall LaVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles on HHSS supports was significantly enhanced compared to conventional large-sized rare earth phosphor fillers. Additionally, by exploiting the reversible fluorescence switching function of the HLE/PS hydrogel, we further developed a high-security multi-layer information encryption platform. Finally, we thoroughly investigated the fluorescence response mechanism of HLE/PS hydrogels to Fe3+ ions. This work not only provides a representative case for the preparation of packed rare-earth fluorescent hydrogels, but also demonstrates their potential as multifunctional smart luminescent materials.

(k) Structure of the HLE/PS hydrogel under polarized light microscopy. (l) The distribution of filler within HLE/PS hydrogel was observed using polarized light microscopy under UV light excitation.

(a) SEM image and (b, c) HRTEM images of LaVO4:Eu3+ filler. (d, e) Photographs of the LE/PS hydrogel under visible light. (f) Distribution of fillers within LE/PS composite fluorescent hydrogel under UV excitation using polarized light microscopy.

(c) Emission spectra of HLE/PS sensor of different sizes after immersed in Fe3+ solution (80 μM) for 8 min. (d) Emission spectra of LE/PS sensor after treatment in Fe3+ solution (0–80 μM). ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Portable and reusable solid-state fluorescence sensors have received much attention in the field of metal ion detection as a superior alternative to traditional liquid-based fluorescence sensors. In this study, a novel HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+/polyacrylamide/sodium alginate (HLE/PS) hydrogel was successfully constructed for portable dynamic fluorescence switching detection of Fe3+ and encryption of security information. The HLE/PS hydrogel was synthesized from HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+ fluorescent nanofillers embedded in a semi-IPN network of polyacrylamide (PAM) and sodium alginate (SA). The HLE/PS hydrogel has demonstrated excellent performance in the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solutions with high selectivity, high sensitivity, fast response (8 min), wide detection range (2–1000 μM), low detection limit (0.78 μM) and ease of use. It can be reused up to five times, underlining its utility and economy as a good performance fluorescence sensor. Notably, the quenching of Fe3+ fluorescence by ultrasmall LaVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles on HHSS supports was significantly enhanced compared to conventional large-sized rare earth phosphor fillers. Additionally, by exploiting the reversible fluorescence switching function of the HLE/PS hydrogel, we further developed a high-security multi-layer information encryption platform. Finally, we thoroughly investigated the fluorescence response mechanism of HLE/PS hydrogels to Fe3+ ions. This work not only provides a representative case for the preparation of packed rare-earth fluorescent hydrogels, but also demonstrates their potential as multifunctional smart luminescent materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron, predominantly existing as Fe3+ in biological systems, is an essential element for human health and environmental processes, playing a pivotal role in numerous biochemical and ecological functions [1,2]. Unlike organic pollutants, Fe3+ persists in the environment and exhibits a tendency to bioaccumulate in flora and fauna, raising significant concerns for human health [3]. Accurate detection and quantification of Fe3+ have thus become a critical focus of research. Conventional analytical methods for Fe3+ detection, including atomic absorption spectrometry [4], chromatography [5], electrochemical techniques [6], and flow injection analysis [7], are often limited by their reliance on expensive instrumentation, complex sample preparation, and poor adaptability to field applications. Furthermore, these methods frequently lack the sensitivity and selectivity required for accurate detection in complex aqueous matrices. These limitations highlight the pressing need for the development of more practical, efficient, and field-deployable methodologies for Fe3+ detection in water resources.

Fluorescence-based detection methods have gained widespread recognition for metal ion sensing due to their simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and user-friendly operation. Numerous fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection have been developed, including carbon dots [8–10], oligo-N-phenylpyrrole derivatives [11], organic frameworks [12,13], and rare earth-based fluorescent sensors [14,15]. However, these probes often suffer from drawbacks such as complex synthesis, poor stability, limited portability, narrow detection ranges, and lack of recyclability. For example, Gong et al. developed rod-shaped La-MOF nanocrystals using recycled poly (ethylene terephthalate) (PET) bottles and La(NO3)3·6H2O [16]. While the La-MOF probe exhibited high selectivity and a low detection limit (0.147 μM) for Fe3+, its linear correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.988) indicated potential inaccuracies in quantitative sensing. Additionally, while solid fluorescent materials have been dispersed in solutions for sensing applications [12,17–19], solid-state fluorescent sensors offer superior stability, portability, and recyclability [20], making them increasingly attractive for practical applications [21–23].

Fluorescent hydrogels have emerged as versatile materials with applications in sensing, information security, bioimaging, and beyond [24–26]. These hydrogels are typically synthesized by integrating luminescent materials into the semi-IPN network of organic hydrogels [27–30]. A promising strategy involves embedding inorganic fluorescent fillers within an organic polymer network, which combines straightforward preparation, robust physicochemical properties, and high luminescence intensity [31–33]. For instance, composite fluorescent hydrogels incorporating NaYF4:Yb/Er nanoparticles have been developed for temperature sensing [34,35]. However, conventional NaYF4:Yb/Er nanoparticles often suffer from issues such as agglomeration and hydroxyl quenching, leading to reduced stability and luminescence performance. Down-conversion inorganic rare-earth phosphors have emerged as superior alternatives due to their efficient energy transfer, intense fluorescence emission, and resistance to hydroxyl quenching [36]. Nevertheless, the synthesis of multifunctional fluorescent hydrogels using these phosphors remains challenging, primarily due to their large size and tendency to agglomerate, which can compromise hydrogel stability [37,38] In our previous work, we successfully synthesized HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+ composite phosphors by anchoring ultrasmall LaVO4:Eu3+ nano-islands onto hierarchical hollow silica spheres (HHSS) via a solvothermal method [39]. These phosphors exhibited strong red emission and exceptional water stability. Building on these findings, we hypothesized that incorporating HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+ as a fluorescent filler within a hydrogel's semi-IPN network could yield a composite material with enhanced fluorescence properties, mechanical robustness, and sensing capabilities.

Here, we report the successful fabrication of a novel HLE/PS composite hydrogel with high fluorescence intensity, achieved by encapsulating HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+ (HLE) fluorescent colloids within a polyacrylamide (PAM) and sodium alginate (SA) polymer network. The HLE/PS hydrogel emits bright red light and demonstrates exceptional performance as a solid-state optical sensor for Fe3+ detection in aqueous solutions. The hydrogel's fluorescence can be dynamically switched on and off using Fe3+ and NH3OHCl, enabling portable and reusable sensing. Moreover, the reversible fluorescence switching capability of the HLE/PS hydrogel facilitates the development of a high-security, multi-level information encryption platform. This study provides a compelling example of the straightforward synthesis of rare earth fluorescent hydrogels and their potential as multifunctional smart luminescent materials.

Acrylamide (AM, 99 %), tetramethylethylenediamine (TE, 99 %), iron (III) nitrate (Fe(NO3)3, 99.9 %), and hydroxylammonium chloride (HC, 98.5 %) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Sodium alginate (SA, AR), N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 99 %), and ammonium persulfate (APS, 99.99 %) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Distilled water (DI) was prepared in the laboratory.

2.4 g of AM was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and stirred magnetically for 5 min at 30 °C in a water bath. Then 0.4 g SA was added to the solution and stirred vigorously for 8 h to obtain a viscous liquid. Then 0.012 g of MBA, 20 μL of TE and 0.024 g of APS were added to the mixture and after 4 min of rapid stirring, the viscous liquid was poured into a mold and polymerized for 8 h at 40 °C to obtain a PAM/SA (PS) hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of HLE/PS composite fluorescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+ (HLE) and LaVO4:Eu3+ (LE) phosphor was prepared in the same way as in our previous work [39]. Under repeated sonication and magnetic stirring, 0.004 g HLE phosphor was dispersed in 20 mL deionized water to form an HLE solution. 2.4 g AM was added to the HLE solution and magnetically stirred for 5 min in a 30 °C water bath. Then 0.4 g of SA was added to the solution and stirred vigorously for 8 h to obtain a viscous liquid. Then 0.012 g MBA, 20 μL TE and 0.024 g APS were added to the mixture with rapid stirring for 4 min and the viscous liquid was poured into a mold and polymerized at 40 °C for 8 h to obtain HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+/polyacrylamide/sodium alginate (HLE/PS) hydrogel. Composite hydrogels with varying HLE content were also synthesized under the same conditions. For comparison, LaVO4:Eu3+/polyacrylamide/sodium alginate (LE/PS) fluorescent hydrogels were prepared using the same protocol.

The crystallinity and phase structure of the samples were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns collected on a Bruker D8 diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. Functional group identification and bonding were determined using a Nicolet IS5 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. Thermal stability was tested in nitrogen (25–800 °C, 10 °C/min) using STA449C (NETZSCH, Germany). Morphological studies were performed using a Philips XL 30 FED scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with a cold field emission source and a JEOL 2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was conducted using a Shimadzu UV-2700i spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 luminescence spectrometer at room temperature.

Tensile tests were performed using a CMT6103 universal testing machine (MTS, USA). Samples with dimensions of 2 mm (thickness) × 20 mm (length) × 10 mm (width) were tested at a constant strain rate of 100 mm/min.

Compression tests were conducted using the same CMT6103 universal testing machine. Samples with dimensions of 10 mm (height) × 10 mm (length) × 10 mm (width) were tested with a 500 N load cell at a speed of 4 mm/min. Compressive strength (σC) was defined as the stress at 70 % compression strain.

Rheological properties of the hydrogels were measured using a HAAKE MARS60 rheometer (Germany) at 25 °C. A 25 mm diameter parallel plate was attached to the transducer, and the gap was set to 1.0 mm. Frequency sweep experiments were conducted from 0.1 to 100 rad/s at a strain of 1.0 %.

A 20 mL Fe3+ ion solution was added to a 50 mL conical flask. The HLE/PS composite fluorescent hydrogel sensor was immersed in the solution and sonicated for 10 min. The emission spectrum of the sensor was then measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HLE/PS sensor cyclic detection performance test
ce:section.ce:para: The HLE/PS sensor (0.2 × 1.0 × 1.0 cm) was immersed in 20 mL of 5 mM Fe3+ ion solution (pH = 6) in a 50 mL conical flask at room temperature. The fluorescence of the sensor was quenched by ultrasonic waves for 8 min, then the sensor was removed, rinsed repeatedly with deionized water and immersed in 0.05 M HC solution and immersed in 50 mM HC solution (pH = 5). Fluorescence recovery was achieved by reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+ by sonication for 30 min. The cycling performance of the HLE/PS sensor was evaluated by repeating the process several times.

A HLE/PS composite fluorescent hydrogel with a patterned shape was prepared in a mold (see Section 2.3 for synthesis details) and flattened into a petri dish. The PS mixture (see Section 2.2 for synthesis details) was then poured over the hydrogel to encapsulate it, followed by polymerization to form a composite hydrogel with fluorescent encrypted information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cyclic fluorescent ‘on-off’ switching design of encrypted information
ce:section.ce:para: A 250 mL beaker containing 60 mL of Fe3+ ion solution (0.005 M) was prepared. The information encryption device was immersed in the solution, and the fluorescence of the encrypted information was quenched by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. The device was then removed, rinsed thoroughly with DI water, and immersed in 0.05 M HC solution. Fluorescence recovery was achieved by reducing Fe3+ via ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, enabling cyclic switching of the encrypted information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of HLE/PS hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process of the HLE/PS composite fluorescent hydrogel is illustrated in Fig. 1a. The HLE fluorescent nanofiller was successfully encapsulated within a semi-IPN network of the PAM/SA (PS) hydrogel, which was synthesized via a one-step free radical polymerization of an acrylamide (AM)/sodium alginate (SA) precursor solution. The composite hydrogel was prepared by polymerizing an aqueous solution containing HLE nanofiller, AM, and SA in the presence of initiators, catalysts, and cross-linkers, resulting in a highly ductile and fluorescent material.

The HLE/PS composite hydrogel was dried, dehydrated, and crushed into a white powder for further characterization. The phase composition and chemical structure of the composite hydrogel were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, PS has no characteristic peak in the XRD pattern due to its amorphous structure. The amorphous peaks of the SiO2 supports within HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+ (HLE) nanofiller coincide with the amorphous peaks of the PS hydrogel in the range of 15° to 30° [39,40]. The diffraction peaks of HLE are in perfect agreement with the standard tetragonal crystal phase of LaVO4 (JCPDS, no. 32-0504), and the major diffraction peaks can be classified as the (101), (200), (112) and (312) crystal planes in the tetragonal crystal structure [41,42]. When the HLE nanofiller is uniformly dispersed within the PS hydrogel, the diffraction peaks corresponding to LaVO4:Eu3+ on the SiO2 support disappear, indicating that the HLE nanofiller is fully encapsulated within the semi-IPN network of the PS hydrogel. The FTIR spectra of the PS hydrogel, HLE nanofiller, and HLE/PS composite are shown in Fig. 1c. The PS hydrogel exhibits characteristic bands at 3332 and 3185 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the primary amine NH. Bands at 2931 and 1453 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration and bending deformation of CH2 , respectively [43,44]. A band at 1657 cm−1 is assigned to the CO stretching vibration, while the band at 1600 cm−1 arises from the bending vibration of the primary amine NH. Additional bands at 1411 and 1318 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration of the CN primary amine, attributed to the interaction of the NH2 group of PAM with the COOH group of SA to form a primary amide bond, and a band at 1120 cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane vibration of NH2 [44]. These observations confirm the successful formation of the PS network. In the FTIR spectrum of the HLE nanofiller, a band at 3433 cm−1 is attributed to the OH group on its surface. The VO band is obscured by SiOSi at 823 cm−1, while the bands of VO4 3− and SiOSi overlap at 446 cm−1 [39,45]. Upon encapsulation of the HLE nanofiller within the PS hydrogel, the functional group bands of the nanofiller disappear in the FTIR spectrum. This result is in agreement with the XRD studies and further confirms the homogeneous dispersion of HLE nanofillers in the HLE/PS hydrogel network.

The thermal stability of the HLE/PS hydrogels was also investigated (Fig. S1). In the low temperature range of 25–150 °C, the hydrogels showed a significant weight loss phenomenon with faster water evaporation and obvious heat absorption peaks, which was caused by the loss of free and bound water in the HLE/PS hydrogel polymer. In the temperature range of 150–560 °C, the weight loss behavior of the hydrogel may be due to the decomposition of organic molecules in the hydrogel matrix. For example, carbon dioxide is released by the decomposition of carboxyl groups and imine is formed by the release of ammonia molecules from amide groups [44,46]. In the temperature range of 560–800 °C, the degradation of the hydrogel is essentially complete and the remaining material may be thermally stable inorganic materials such as oxides or carbon black. At this point, the samples show thermal stability and the DSC and TGA curves tend to be stable. As the amount of HLE filler in the hydrogel was only 0.02 wt%, it was not possible to analyze this filler in the heat loss study. However, it is possible to infer the formation of a semi-IPN network of the PS hydrogel.

The morphological and structural characteristics of the HLE nanofiller, PS hydrogel, and HLE/PS composite were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and polarizing microscopy. The HLE fluorescent nanofiller retained the morphology of its hierarchical hollow silica sphere (HHSS) support, featuring an ordered arrangement of small hollow silica nanospheres that assemble into larger hollow spheres with a predominant particle size of 90–150 nm. Additionally, ultra-small LaVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles, ranging from 2 to 3 nm in size, were uniformly dispersed on the surfaces of these spheres (Fig. 2a–c). The lyophilized hydrogels were examined using SEM to elucidate their microstructural features. The PAM-SA (PS) hydrogel displayed a uniform honeycomb-like porous structure (Fig. 2d–e), with higher magnification revealing a smooth surface morphology resulting from the dense entanglement of PAM and SA polymer chains (Fig. 2f). Upon incorporation of a small amount of HLE nanofiller, the composite fluorescent hydrogel maintained this uniform honeycomb porous structure (Fig. 2g–h). Encapsulated HLE nanofillers, appearing as large convex particles approximately 300 nm in size, were observed on the smooth network skeleton, with the surfaces of these particles also exhibiting a smooth texture (Fig. 2i). As shown in Fig. 2j–k, the surface of the composite hydrogel appeared smooth and flat, with the hydrogel network structure clearly visible under magnification using a polarizing light microscope. When exposed to UV light, the hydrogel exhibited numerous uniformly dispersed red particles, indicative of the red fluorescence emitted by the excited HLE nanofiller (Fig. 2l). Additionally, as demonstrated in Fig. S2 and Video S1, the fluorescent thin layer formed by adequately dehydrating the HLE/PS hydrogel displayed excellent flexibility and shape memory properties. Combined with the XRD and FTIR results, these findings confirm the successful encapsulation of the HLE nanofiller within the PS hydrogel, resulting in a uniform composite fluorescent hydrogel.

The morphological and structural characteristics of the HLE nanofiller, PS hydrogel, and HLE/PS composite were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and polarizing microscopy. The HLE fluorescent nanofiller retained the morphology of its hierarchical hollow silica sphere (HHSS) support, featuring an ordered arrangement of small hollow silica nanospheres that assemble into larger hollow spheres with a predominant particle size of 90–150 nm. Additionally, ultra-small LaVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles, ranging from 2 to 3 nm in size, were uniformly dispersed on the surfaces of these spheres (Fig. 2a–c). The lyophilized hydrogels were examined using SEM to elucidate their microstructural features. The PAM-SA (PS) hydrogel displayed a uniform honeycomb-like porous structure (Fig. 2d–e), with higher magnification revealing a smooth surface morphology resulting from the dense entanglement of PAM and SA polymer chains (Fig. 2f). Upon incorporation of a small amount of HLE nanofiller, the composite fluorescent hydrogel maintained this uniform honeycomb porous structure (Fig. 2g–h). Encapsulated HLE nanofillers, appearing as large convex particles approximately 300 nm in size, were observed on the smooth network skeleton, with the surfaces of these particles also exhibiting a smooth texture (Fig. 2i). As shown in Fig. 2j–k, the surface of the composite hydrogel appeared smooth and flat, with the hydrogel network structure clearly visible under magnification using a polarizing light microscope. When exposed to UV light, the hydrogel exhibited numerous uniformly dispersed red particles, indicative of the red fluorescence emitted by the excited HLE nanofiller (Fig. 2l). Additionally, as demonstrated in Fig. S2 and Video S1, the fluorescent thin layer formed by adequately dehydrating the HLE/PS hydrogel displayed excellent flexibility and shape memory properties. Combined with the XRD and FTIR results, these findings confirm the successful encapsulation of the HLE nanofiller within the PS hydrogel, resulting in a uniform composite fluorescent hydrogel.

Conventional rare earth phosphors often undergo significant agglomeration after high-temperature annealing. For instance, we synthesized tetragonal-phase LaVO4:Eu3+ phosphor using a hydrothermal method and subsequently annealed it at 800 °C [39]. While individual LaVO4:Eu3+ particles are approximately 30 nm in size, high-temperature annealing caused them to agglomerate into clusters tens of microns or larger, making dispersion challenging (Fig. 3a–c).

The LE/PS fluorescent hydrogel, prepared by encapsulating LaVO4:Eu3+ filler within the PAM/SA hydrogel network, appeared similar to the HLE/PS composite hydrogel under visible light (Fig. 3d–e). However, under 265 nm UV light, the large LaVO4:Eu3+ phosphor particles dispersed throughout the hydrogel network became visible under polarized light microscopy (Fig. 3f). In contrast, the HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+ phosphor filler, with a size of approximately 100 nm, was uniformly distributed within the PS hydrogel (Fig. 2l). The tendency of LaVO4:Eu3+ phosphor fillers to agglomerate adversely affects the mechanical, luminescent, and application properties of the resulting LE/PS hydrogel, highlighting a limitation in the synthesis of filler-based fluorescent hydrogels as reported by some researchers [34,35,47]. To address this issue, we developed a composite phosphor by anchoring ultra-small rare earth phosphor particles onto HHSS supports, which was then used as a filler to synthesize the composite fluorescent hydrogel. This approach effectively resolves the critical challenge of agglomeration, enabling the production of hydrogels with enhanced properties.

The mechanical properties of the hydrogels were systematically evaluated through tensile, compression, and rheological tests. Fig. 4a presents the tensile stress-strain curves of hydrogels with varying HLE nanofiller content. The PS hydrogel exhibited excellent ductility, with a fracture stress of 109.52 kPa and a fracture strain of 470.97 %. As the concentration of HLE nanofiller in the PS hydrogel network increased (0, 0.02, 0.04, and 0.06 wt%), the fracture stress of the composite hydrogels showed a slight but consistent upward trend, while the fracture strain remained largely unchanged. Notably, the fracture stress of HLE/PS (0.06 wt%) increased to 118.95 kPa, representing an 8.6 % enhancement compared to the pristine PS hydrogel, while maintaining a high fracture strain of 465.70 %. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the assembled HLE/PS composite fluorescent hydrogel demonstrates excellent cyclic stretching and shape recovery properties. The dynamic behavior of this process is captured under visible and ultraviolet light in Videos S2 and S3, respectively. Although the water content of the PS and HLE/PS hydrogels is similar, their hysteresis properties differ significantly. This suggests that ductile interpenetrating network hydrogels contribute minimally to energy dissipation, whereas homogeneous multi-networks with diverse supramolecular interactions can effectively dissipate energy.

The mechanical properties of the hydrogels were systematically evaluated through tensile, compression, and rheological tests. Fig. 4a presents the tensile stress-strain curves of hydrogels with varying HLE nanofiller content. The PS hydrogel exhibited excellent ductility, with a fracture stress of 109.52 kPa and a fracture strain of 470.97 %. As the concentration of HLE nanofiller in the PS hydrogel network increased (0, 0.02, 0.04, and 0.06 wt%), the fracture stress of the composite hydrogels showed a slight but consistent upward trend, while the fracture strain remained largely unchanged. Notably, the fracture stress of HLE/PS (0.06 wt%) increased to 118.95 kPa, representing an 8.6 % enhancement compared to the pristine PS hydrogel, while maintaining a high fracture strain of 465.70 %. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the assembled HLE/PS composite fluorescent hydrogel demonstrates excellent cyclic stretching and shape recovery properties. The dynamic behavior of this process is captured under visible and ultraviolet light in Videos S2 and S3, respectively. Although the water content of the PS and HLE/PS hydrogels is similar, their hysteresis properties differ significantly. This suggests that ductile interpenetrating network hydrogels contribute minimally to energy dissipation, whereas homogeneous multi-networks with diverse supramolecular interactions can effectively dissipate energy.

The HLE/PS hydrogel, rich in hydrogen and coordination bonds, dissipates substantial energy through the dissociation of these supramolecular interactions under external force. The compressive strength of the HLE/PS hydrogel exhibited a positive correlation with HLE content (Fig. 4c). This enhancement is attributed to the interaction between the hydroxyl groups on the HLE nanofiller surface and the amino groups of the acrylamide, which promotes cross-linking within the HLE/PS structure. The maximum compressive strength achieved by the hydrogel with 0.06 wt% HLE content was 570.5 kPa. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4d, the storage modulus (G′) of the hydrogels increased with higher HLE nanofiller content. The storage modulus of the hydrogel with 0.06 wt% HLE nanofiller reached 9905 Pa, representing a 2.14-fold improvement over the PS hydrogel without HLE nanofiller, which had a storage modulus of 4629 Pa. These results indicate that the incorporation of HLE nanofillers into the PS hydrogel network significantly enhances its mechanical strength through cross-linking interactions. Although the mechanical properties of the HLE/PS hydrogel are not particularly impressive, this is sufficient to warrant its exploration as a fundamental application in the field of optical sensors and information encryption.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the hydrogel samples are presented in Fig. 5a. The fluorescent hydrogel containing HLE nanofillers exhibits two distinct peaks near 230 nm and 290 nm, corresponding to charge transfer from O2− ions to V5+ ions in the LaVO4 host [48]. In contrast, no absorption peaks were observed in the PS hydrogel [49,50]. The luminescence behavior of the HLE/PS hydrogel was further investigated using excitation-emission spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5b, a broad and intense band near 300 nm is observed, attributed to charge transfer (CT) from OV bonds and energy transfer (ET) to Eu3+ within the HLE nanofiller [37,39]. This observation aligns with the UV–Vis spectra. Additionally, the band near 385 nm arises from the excited state transitions of Eu3+ electrons [39]. Strong red emission at 621 nm is produced by the 5D₀ → 7F2 transitions of Eu3+, while the 5D₀ → 7F₁ and 5D₀ → 7F4 transitions are detected at 594 nm and 703 nm, respectively. As the HLE nanofiller content increased from 0 to 0.06 wt%, the red emission intensity at 621 nm within the hydrogel progressively increased (Fig. 5c), accompanied by a corresponding rise in photoluminescence (PL) intensity (Fig. 5d). These results show that HLE still has good luminescence performance in the semi-IPN network of the PS hydrogel, and there is no obvious hydroxyl quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ detection based on HLE/PS sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ is an essential trace element critical for cellular and biological metabolism. Its deficiency can lead to anemia, while excess levels may impose a burden on the liver, potentially causing conditions such as cirrhosis. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ in drinking water and aquatic environments is of significant importance [51]. Fluorescence sensing, renowned for its simplicity, efficiency, and cost-effectiveness, is widely favored for Fe3+ detection [52–54]. In our previous study, we demonstrated that an HHSS@LaVO4:Eu3+ (HLE) colloidal aqueous solution exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ detection in drinking water [39].

However, its detection limit (4.59 μM) was insufficiently low, the fluorescence response time was slow, and the system lacked recyclability. In this study, we investigated the effects of nine metal ion solutions (60 μM) Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Mg2+, K+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ on the emission intensity of the HLE/PS fluorescence sensor. The results revealed that Fe3+ exhibited a pronounced selective quenching effect on the fluorescence of the HLE/PS sensor (Fig. S3). Interestingly, the emission intensity of the HLE/PS hydrogel increased by 1.4 % after treatment with a Ca2+ solution. This fluorescence enhancement can be attributed to the influence of Ca2+ on the local structure and electronic environment of LaVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles within the HLE filler, improving the energy and charge transfer efficiency of VO4 3− → Eu3+ and thereby enhancing the emission intensity of Eu3+ [36,55,56].

We further explored the performance of the HLE/PS solid-state fluorescence sensor for detecting Fe3+ in drinking water. The HLE/PS sensor was immersed in a Fe3+ solution (80 μM), and emission spectra were recorded after varying durations of ultrasonic treatment (Fig. 6a). As the response time increased, the intensity of the red emission peak of Eu3+ at 621 nm gradually decreased. Within 3 min, the fluorescence intensity dropped sharply by 17.5 %, which can be attributed to the domain-limiting effect of the PS hydrogel network on the HLE nanofiller. This effect requires Fe3+ to traverse the hydrogel membrane to bind with the HLE nanofiller, preventing abrupt fluorescence quenching over short periods. When the response time was extended to 8 min, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 34.3 %, with a further reduction to 34.6 % at 10 min. These results indicate that ultrasonication accelerates the diffusion of Fe3+ from the solution into the HLE/PS hydrogel sensor, facilitating its adsorption onto the HLE surface through electrostatic interactions and resulting in fluorescence quenching. Based on these findings, an 8 min saturation time for the Fe3+ quenching reaction was deemed optimal (Fig. 6b).

To determine the optimal sensor size, we prepared HLE/PS sensors of varying dimensions and immersed them in a Fe3+ solution (80 μM) after 8 min of sonication. The emission spectra of the sensors were subsequently recorded. As shown in Fig. 6c, the fluorescence intensity of the 0.2 × 1.0 × 1.0 cm sensor decreased by 20.6 % following the quenching reaction. The quenching effect diminished with increasing sensor size, with only a 2.3 % reduction in fluorescence intensity observed for the 0.2 × 2.5 × 2.5 cm sensor.

Under identical reaction conditions, smaller sensor sizes facilitated greater diffusion of Fe3+ into the sensor, leading to a more pronounced quenching effect. Consequently, the 0.2 × 1.0 × 1.0 cm HLE/PS sensor is identified as the most suitable for optimal performance. As a control experiment, we prepared a 0.2 × 1.0 × 1.0 cm LaVO4:Eu3+/polyacrylamide/sodium alginate (LE/PS) fluorescent hydrogel sensor and measured its emission spectra after immersion in Fe3+ solutions of varying concentrations, followed by ultrasonication (Fig. 6d). The fluorescence intensity values at 621 nm were linearly fitted to the Fe3+ concentration, yielding a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.966 (Fig. 6e). For optical sensors, a correlation coefficient (R2) greater than 0.99 is typically required to ensure accuracy, as lower values indicate significant errors in the fitting equation used for Fe3+ detection. Thus, the LE/PS fluorescent hydrogel is unsuitable as a reliable sensor for quantifying Fe3+ content in water. This limitation arises from the large size of the LaVO4:Eu3+ filler within the hydrogel, which restricts Fe3+ complexation to quenching only the surface fluorescence, leaving the internal fluorescence largely unaffected.

Fig. 7a illustrates the effect of Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence quenching of the HLE/PS sensor. As the Fe3+ concentration increased, the color of the sensor under 265 nm UV light gradually darkened from a vivid bright red, indicating the effective quenching effect of Fe3+. In addition, the HLE/PS sensor size did not undergo significant deformation and swelling after Fe3+ quenching. The HLE/PS sensor was immersed in Fe3+ solutions of varying concentrations (2–1000 μM) for 8 min, and the emission spectra were recorded (Fig. 7b, d). The intensity of the red emission peak at 621 nm decreased continuously with increasing Fe3+ concentration. A strong linear relationship was observed in the concentration range of 2–80 μM, with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.994, as described by Eq. (1): I₀/I = 0.9853 + 0.0084 [Fe3+] (Fig. 7b, c). This demonstrates the superior sensing accuracy of the HLE/PS sensor compared to the LE/PS sensor, which exhibited a lower correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.966). The limit of detection (LOD), a critical parameter in sensory analysis [20], was calculated according to the IUPAC standard (LOD = 3σ/k) and found to be 0.78 μM.

This value is significantly lower than the permissible Fe3+ concentration in drinking water (5.3 μM, Standards for Drinking Water Quality, GB 5749-2022), where σ represents the standard deviation of the fluorescence measurements of the blank (five replicates) and k is the slope of the fitted linear curve. Additionally, a good linear fit was observed in the Fe3+ concentration range of 80–1000 μM, as described by Eq. (2): I₀/I = 1.387 + 0.0031 [Fe3+], with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.997 (Fig. 7d, e). These equations enable the quantitative determination of Fe3+ concentration in unknown aqueous solutions based on the emission intensity ratio.

The reproducibility of a luminescent sensor is a crucial criterion for practical applications. The reproducibility of the HLE/PS sensor was evaluated by measuring the emission spectrum after each recycling experiment. As shown in Fig. 8a, no significant change in photoluminescence (PL) intensity was observed, and the sensor could be recycled up to five times. To further validate its performance, the cycled HLE/PS sensor was re-immersed in a Fe3+ solution (60 μM) to quench the fluorescence, and the emission spectrum data were fitted to Eq. (1) (Fig. 8b). The calculated Fe3+ concentration was 58.99 μM, with a recovery value of 98.32 %. The excellent cycling performance of the sensor is attributed to the robust mechanical properties of the hydrogel. However, repeated sonication can damage the hydrogel network structure, potentially affecting its long-term cycling performance. The analytical performance of the HLE/PS sensor, including its wide linear detection range and low detection limit, is comparable to or better than previously reported optical sensors (Table S1), highlighting its potential for applications in biomedical and environmental systems. Previous studies have utilized rare earth inorganic fluorescent solutions for detecting metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Fe3+) in water. However, these sensors often suffer from limitations such as high detection thresholds, narrow concentration ranges, poor stability, and lack of convenience and recyclability [14,19,55,57,58]. In contrast, the HLE/PS sensor demonstrates significant advantages over these predecessors, offering improved sensitivity, stability, and reusability.

Despite the above advantages of the HLE/PS sensor, there are some limitations: (1) limited cycle life, although the sensor showed good reproducibility over five cycles, long-term use (beyond the test conditions) may degrade performance due to damage to the hydrogel structure caused by repeated sonication; (2) potential interferences in complex matrices, this study focuses on controlled solutions for Fe3 + detection. Actual samples (e.g. biological fluids or wastewater) may contain interfering species that affect the accuracy; (3) dependence on hydrogel integrity-mechanical stability is critical, and environmental factors (pH, temperature or ionic strength) may affect hydrogel properties and sensor reliability.

The importance of information security in commercial, military, and daily activities cannot be overstated. A promising fluorescence encryption technique has been developed based on a multi-level information encryption platform utilizing ionic redox reactions. To further explore the optical applications of the HLE/PS hydrogel, we leveraged its fluorescence-responsive properties toward Fe3+ and NH3OHCl to design a series of anti-counterfeiting platforms for information encryption and decryption. Initially, the HLE/PS hydrogel exhibits strong red emission under 265 nm UV light, while appearing indistinguishable from the PS hydrogel under visible light. Using a mold, HLE/PS hydrogel with encoded information patterns was prepared and embedded within a PS hydrogel matrix to create an encryption platform with concealed fluorescent information patterns. The preparation process is detailed in the Experimental section. Under visible light, the platform is morphologically and visually identical to a standard PS hydrogel. However, the hidden information patterns become visible only under UV excitation at a specific wavelength.

As shown in Fig. 9a, we designed six encryption platforms featuring fluorescent patterns of a “penguin”, “butterfly”, “dolphin” and “star”. These platforms are simple to design, cost-effective, and provide a one-level encryption effect. Building on this one-level encryption, we utilized Fe3+ to completely quench the fluorescence of the information patterns, creating a two-level encryption platform. Even under UV light, the encrypted information remains concealed. Only by reducing Fe3+ with NH3OHCl to reactivate the fluorescence can the encoded information be revealed under UV light. Fig. 9b illustrates the encryption and decryption process of a “heart” shaped two-level encryption platform. Compared to the one-level platform, the two-level encryption significantly enhances information security. Moreover, the platform design is straightforward, requiring no complex procedures, expensive reagents, or specialized equipment. In conclusion, the HLE/PS multi-level information encryption platform offers a promising strategy for applying fluorescent hydrogels in information security. This approach holds significant potential for industrial applications in military and commercial sectors, providing a robust and cost-effective solution for advanced information encryption.

Based on the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the HLE nanofiller, the direct electronic band gap (E) was estimated to be 3.46 eV (Figs. S4 and S5), consistent with values reported for LaVO4:Eu3+ phosphors in the literature [59]. The valence band energy (Ev) of LaVO4 in the HLE nanofiller, calculated from XPS spectra, was approximately −4.65 eV below the vacuum level (Fig. S6). Consequently, its conduction band energy (E
c ) was determined to be Ev + E = −1.19 eV (Fig. 10 ). The standard reduction potential for Fe3+ is E
0 = 0.77 V vs. NHE [59]. At pH = 5.0 (the experimental condition of this study), the actual reduction potential is E
Fe3+ = E
0 − 0.059 × pH = 0.47 V. The electrochemical reduction potential corresponds to a redox Fermi energy of E
Fe3+,red = −4.5 eV − E
Fe3+ = −4.97 eV. Since the conduction band energy level of LaVO4 (E
c ) exceeds the reduction Fermi level of Fe3+ (E
c > E
Fe3+,red ), and the abundance of hydroxyl groups on the HLE surface enhances its negative potential, Fe3+ forms strong complexes with the nanofiller. As a result, most of the energetic electrons in the LaVO4 matrix, excited into its conduction band by UV light, are transferred to nearby Fe3+ ions. Only a small fraction of electrons relax back to the ground state or transfer energy to Eu3+ luminescence centers. This photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process leads to photoluminescence (PL) quenching in the HLE/PS hydrogel.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the Fe3+ solution shows no absorption at wavelengths longer than 550 nm but exhibits a distinct absorption peak in the 200–400 nm range, overlapping with that of the HLE nanofiller (Fig. S7). This indicates that Fe3+ cannot absorb photons emitted by Eu3+, suggesting that energy and charge transfer from VO4 3− to Fe3+ is the primary mechanism underlying PL quenching in the HLE/PS hydrogel. Furthermore, Fig. S8 demonstrates that Fe2+ does not quench the fluorescence of the HLE/PS hydrogel, as the reduction Fermi energy level of Fe2+/Fe(s) is approximately −3.6 eV [60,61], which is higher than the conduction band energy of LaVO4. The standard oxidation potential of NH3OHCl (hydroxylamine hydrochloride, HH) is E
0 = 0.05 V vs. NHE. At pH = 5.0, the actual oxidation potential is E
HH = E
0 –0.059 × pH = 0.03 V. The electrochemical oxidation potential corresponds to a redox Fermi energy of E
HH, oxid = −4.5 eV − E
HH = −4.53 eV. Since the oxidation Fermi level of NH3OHCl (−4.53 eV) is higher than the reduction Fermi level of Fe3+ (−4.97 eV), Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ by NH3OHCl in the HLE/PS hydrogel. This reduction weakens the PET process, restoring energy transfer to Eu3+ and reactivating the fluorescence of the HLE/PS hydrogel (Fig. 10). The reaction between NH3OHCl and Fe3+ can be described as follows:
2Fe

/ HLE +
2NH 3

+ HLE

In summary, we successfully developed a multifunctional HLE/PS fluorescent hydrogel through a straightforward synthesis approach. The HLE/PS optical sensor exhibits exceptional performance for detecting Fe3+ in aqueous solutions, demonstrating high selectivity, sensitivity, a rapid response time, a broad detection range, a low detection limit, and ease of use. Its reusability over five cycles further underscores its practical utility as a high-performance fluorescence sensor. This work not only elucidates the fluorescence response mechanism of the HLE/PS sensor to Fe3+ but also establishes a highly effective optical anti-counterfeiting platform for information encryption and decryption. By leveraging specific wavelength excitation and chemical quenching-activated fluorescence, we created a multi-level encryption platform that significantly enhances information security. This study provides a simple and viable strategy for synthesizing rare earth fluorescent hydrogels with excellent optical properties and stability, offering a promising foundation for the next generation of functional and practical optical applications of fluorescent hydrogels. Despite its promising results, the study has several limitations, such as the long-term stability of HLE/PS fluorescent hydrogels, the sensitivity of Fe3+ detection in real complex environments, and the practicality of information encryption which warrant further investigation.
